_meta.purpose: Drives the homepage Work list (// SYS.03) and the Case Study detail page. Each entry is one mission dossier.
consumedBy: Homepage > Work section (work-row + sticky preview monitor)
Case Study page (header, hero console, body, before/after, quote, next-link)
_meta.ordering: Array order is the display order in the Work list (idx 0..n).
_meta.routing: slug is the ?id= value used by the Case Study page and the work-row href.

correctness.facts: Names, taglines, tech stacks, links, descriptions, and metrics are real, sourced from profile.json and the live GitHub profile.
correctness.quotes: quote.* are FIRST-PERSON author design-principles by Amit Rana, NOT third-party testimonials. Replace with real client/user quotes when available.
correctness.beforeAfter: result.before / result.after are qualitative without-it / with-it capability contrasts, not measured client metrics.
correctness.pendingAssets: image is null where no screenshot exists yet in /public/projects (coherence, mcp-sentry, git-compass, glassbox, ghostframes).
correctness.years: year is approximate for hookpilot and javascript-verse; private engagements use "Private" in place of a date.
correctness.privateWork: skinbattle and felis are real but private. isPrivate and private flags are set. skinbattle is a Counter-Strike skin-trading platform and online casino (Plinko, Mines, Cases, Battles, Crash built from scratch) at 5k concurrent users, per the owner; Amit's role was Frontend Engineer and its quote is his own. felis was built for research scholar Vanshika Tyagi (RAG over her research papers); its quote is from her, with verified: false until exact wording and consent are confirmed.
correctness.displayMetrics: When a metric has value: null and a `display` string, render it as static text (no count-up). Used for private engagements where numeric KPIs are not public.

caseStudies.name: Hookpilot

row.blurb: Pre-commit checks were slow, inconsistent, and easy to skip. An open-source Git hook manager that makes the right checks automatic.

caseStudies.tagline: Teams kept skipping inconsistent pre-commit checks. I built a flexible Git hook manager that makes the right checks automatic, now 50k+ weekly downloads on npm.
caseStudies.context: Hookpilot is an open-source Git hook manager. It ships a library of ready-to-use hooks (linting, formatting, testing, security checks, commit-message validation, branch protection, large-file checks) plus an interactive CLI for adding, restoring, listing, and removing them.

problem.head: Pre-commit checks were slow, inconsistent across machines, and trivially skipped, so standards only got enforced after CI, long after the commit.
problem.body: Every project reinvented its hook setup by hand. Configs drifted between clones, new contributors started with nothing wired up, and a quick override let anyone bypass the gates entirely.
problem.before: Hand-rolled hooks · drift across clones · checks skipped freely

approach.intro: I built a zero-config CLI that wires standard hooks across the full Git lifecycle from one versioned config, with first-class support for custom shell and Node scripts.

cards.heading: Predefined hook library
cards.body: Ready-made hooks for pre-commit, commit-msg, pre-push, post-merge, and post-checkout tasks.

cards.heading: One versioned config
cards.body: hooks-config.json is committed once; every clone inherits the same gates with a single init.

result.head: A single init wires the whole team's gates, and the package now sees 50k+ weekly downloads on npm.

quote.text: I wanted hooks to be the easy path, not a chore you bypass. One config, committed once, and the whole team is on the same gates.
quote.name: Amit Rana

caseStudies.name: Coherence

row.blurb: Docs rot the moment code changes. A Claude Code plugin that detects documentation drift and proposes validated, surgical patches.

caseStudies.tagline: Docs rot the moment code changes. I built a Claude Code plugin that detects documentation drift and proposes surgical patches through a validated two-stage LLM pipeline.
caseStudies.context: Coherence runs as Claude Code hooks and slash commands. It watches for code changing without its docs and proposes patches, with every team-distributable surface living as committed files and no backend, ever.

problem.head: Documentation drifts silently: code changes, the docs that describe it do not, and the gap is only found when someone is misled by it.
problem.body: Naively asking an LLM to fix the docs is unsafe: it hallucinates, rewrites too much, and can be steered by injected text. There was no trustworthy way to let an agent touch documentation unsupervised.
problem.before: Docs drift unnoticed · risky blind LLM edits · no safe auto-apply

approach.intro: I split the work into a two-stage pipeline with deterministic validation between the model and the filesystem, and gated auto-apply behind a per-developer trust ledger.

cards.heading: Two-stage pipeline
cards.body: Stage 1 selects the canonical source; Stage 2 writes a patch that must pass format, apply, change-class, line-ratio, injection, hallucination, and asserts checks.

cards.heading: Graduated trust gate
cards.body: Auto-applies only above a 0.85 trust score, decayed on a 30-day half-life, and never for destructive or frontmatter changes.

result.head: An agent can keep docs in sync safely, with eight validation engines and cosign-signed releases standing between the model and your repo.

quote.text: I won't let a model write to a repo on trust alone. Every patch has to survive deterministic checks before it ever lands.
quote.name: Amit Rana

caseStudies.name: mcp-sentry

row.blurb: MCP servers ship with quiet security holes. A static-analysis linter covering all eight OWASP MCP Top 10 categories, graded A to F in CI.

caseStudies.tagline: MCP servers ship with quiet security holes. I built a static analysis linter that covers all eight OWASP MCP Top 10 categories and grades a project A to F in CI.
caseStudies.context: mcp-sentry is a static-analysis security linter for TypeScript Model Context Protocol servers. It integrates with CI via SARIF, PR comments, and a public Shields.io badge.

problem.head: MCP servers expose tools to agents, and a mislabeled or injectable tool can hand an attacker command execution, with nothing flagging it in review.
problem.body: There was no MCP-aware linter. Generic scanners miss MCP-specific risks like a read-only-named tool that mutates state, or untrusted input flowing into a shell call across function boundaries.
problem.before: No MCP-aware scanning · injection risks unflagged · intent mislabeling invisible

approach.intro: I mapped the OWASP MCP Top 10 to concrete checks, added inter-procedural taint tracking for command injection, and made the whole thing CI-native with a gradeable threshold.

cards.heading: Inter-procedural taint tracking
cards.body: Follows untrusted input across functions to catch command injection that single-function scanners miss.

cards.heading: Intent-subversion detection
cards.body: Flags read-only-named tools that actually mutate state, a classic MCP trust violation.

result.head: One scan grades an MCP server A to F across all eight OWASP MCP categories, fails CI on a threshold, and posts a public badge.

quote.text: Security tooling has to live where the work happens. mcp-sentry fails the build and posts the grade, so nobody has to remember to run it.
quote.name: Amit Rana

caseStudies.name: GitCompass

row.blurb: Repo risk hides in commit history. A local-first analytics tool surfacing hotspots, burnout, and bus-factor risk, with plain-English AI querying.

caseStudies.tagline: Repo risk hides in commit history. I built a local-first analytics tool that surfaces hotspots, burnout signals, and bus-factor risk, with plain-English AI querying.
caseStudies.context: GitCompass reads commit history to surface high-churn hotspots, velocity spikes, contributor burnout signals, bus-factor risk, and onboarding guidance. It ships as a CLI and a visual dashboard, and runs entirely on your machine.

problem.head: The signals that predict delivery risk, churn, single-owner files, after-hours spikes, are buried in Git history that nobody has time to mine.
problem.body: Existing analytics meant shipping your repo to a cloud service, an immediate non-starter for private code, and still answered fixed questions rather than the one you actually had.
problem.before: Risk signals buried in history · cloud upload required · fixed dashboards only

approach.intro: I built a modular monorepo: an analytics core, a CLI, and a dashboard, with an AI query layer that answers questions in plain English, all running locally.

cards.heading: Local-first by design
cards.body: Everything runs on your machine. No account, no cloud, no telemetry, so it works on private repos.

cards.heading: Plain-English querying
cards.body: Ask the repo a question in natural language; multi-SDK support over the local analytics.

result.head: Hotspots, burnout, and bus-factor risk surface from one local scan, queryable in plain English, with nothing leaving the machine.

quote.text: Analytics shouldn't cost you your privacy. GitCompass never phones home, and you can just ask it what you want to know.
quote.name: Amit Rana

caseStudies.name: Glassbox

row.blurb: You can't improve an agent you can't see. A local, read-only observer that turns a Claude Code transcript into a six-metric behavioural report.

caseStudies.tagline: You can't improve an agent you can't see. I built a local, read-only observer that turns a Claude Code transcript into a six-metric behavioural report.
caseStudies.context: Glassbox reads a Claude Code JSONL transcript and renders a single self-contained HTML report with six behavioural metrics, a session timeline, and a harness-health scorecard. It makes no network calls and never controls the agent.

problem.head: Agent sessions are opaque: when a run goes sideways, there's no readable account of what the agent actually did or why.
problem.body: The raw transcript is a wall of JSONL. There was no way to see whether an agent over-searched, declared victory early, skipped verification, or looped, the patterns that separate a healthy run from a wasteful one.
problem.before: Sessions opaque · raw JSONL only · no behavioural signal

approach.intro: I built a zero-dependency, read-only observer that scores six behaviours and renders one portable HTML report, intentionally built under its own harness to illustrate what it measures.

cards.heading: Six behavioural metrics
cards.body: Grep-vs-semantic ratio, early-victory detection, verification density, scope overreach, continuity, and loop detection.

cards.heading: Safe by construction
cards.body: Read-only and offline: it auto-discovers the latest transcript, redacts secrets, and never controls the agent.

result.head: A messy transcript becomes one self-contained HTML report scoring six behaviours, with zero network calls and full secret redaction.

quote.text: If you can't measure how an agent behaves, you can't fix it. Glassbox stays read-only and offline, so observing never changes the run.
quote.name: Amit Rana

caseStudies.name: Ghostframes

row.blurb: Hand-maintained skeleton loaders drift from their components. Zero-config skeletons generated from real components, gated by CI.

caseStudies.tagline: Hand-maintained skeleton loaders rot as components change. I built zero-config skeletons generated from real components, gated by CI.
caseStudies.context: Ghostframes is a React skeleton-loader library shipped as a single public package, @ghostframes/runtime. It captures skeleton manifests from real components at build time and enforces their quality in CI.

problem.head: Skeleton loaders are written by hand and drift from the components they mimic, so they decay into the wrong shape over time.
problem.body: Maintaining skeletons manually is tedious and error-prone, and nothing catches when a skeleton stops matching its component, so loading states quietly degrade.
problem.before: Hand-written skeletons · drift from components · no quality gate

approach.intro: I generated skeletons from real components at build time and added CI gates that enforce schema, key coverage, an invalid-entry budget, and artifact size.

cards.heading: Build-time capture
cards.body: AutoSkeleton and generateStaticBlueprint produce loader artifacts from the real component tree.

cards.heading: CI quality gates
cards.body: ghostframes validate, diff, and report enforce schema, coverage, and size on every change.

result.head: Skeletons are generated from the real components and held in line by CI gates, so loading states stay accurate with no hand-maintenance.

quote.text: A skeleton should come from the component, not a second copy you maintain by hand. Generate it, then let CI keep it honest.
quote.name: Amit Rana

caseStudies.name: JavaScript-Verse

row.blurb: Trying JS or React usually means a sandbox setup. An in-browser playground that runs real code, real npm modules, and React, with no install.

caseStudies.tagline: Trying JavaScript or React usually means a sandbox setup. I built an in-browser playground that runs real code, real npm modules, and React, with no install.
caseStudies.context: JavaScript-Verse is a browser-based code execution environment and markdown editor for writing and running JavaScript and React, with side-by-side docs, a cheatsheet, and code export.

problem.head: Experimenting with JavaScript or React meant a local toolchain or a limited sandbox that couldn't pull real npm modules or render components.
problem.body: Running untrusted code in the browser safely, transpiling JSX and modern syntax, and resolving imports without a server are each non-trivial, so most playgrounds punt on one of them.
problem.before: Local setup to experiment · no real npm modules · JSX unsupported

approach.intro: I solved safe execution with iframe isolation, advanced syntax with in-browser Babel transpilation, and imports with in-browser bundling.

cards.heading: Sandboxed execution
cards.body: Code runs inside an isolated iframe with live preview, so arbitrary code is safe to run.

cards.heading: Real modules + JSX
cards.body: In-browser Babel transpiles JSX and modern syntax; in-browser bundling resolves any npm import.

result.head: Anyone can write and run JavaScript or React in the browser, pull real npm modules, and export their code, with nothing to install.
after: Runs in the browser, no install

quote.text: The fastest way to learn something is to run it. I wanted real JavaScript and React, real modules, with nothing to install first.
quote.name: Amit Rana

caseStudies.name: SkinBattle.gg

row.blurb: Counter-Strike skin trading platform and online casino. Plinko, Mines, Cases, Battles, and Crash, built from scratch, at 5k concurrent users.

caseStudies.tagline: A Counter-Strike skin trading platform and online casino, with games like Plinko, Mines, Cases, Battles, and Crash built from scratch, serving 5k concurrent users.
caseStudies.context: SkinBattle.gg is a Counter-Strike skin trading platform and online casino. The team built its games, Plinko, Mines, Cases, Battles, Crash and more, from scratch, with live multiplayer over WebSockets, serving up to 5k concurrent users. I worked on the frontend, building the real-time game interfaces and client state.

problem.head: A casino platform has to keep fast, animated game interfaces in perfect sync with live server state for thousands of players at once, with money on every round.
problem.body: Games like Crash and Battles update many times a second and resolve in real time. The frontend has to render those updates smoothly, stay consistent with the server's outcome, and hold up at 5k concurrent users without stutter or desync.
problem.before: Casino games from scratch · live outcomes · 5k concurrent players

approach.intro: I built the game frontends around a WebSocket-driven state layer with Redux Toolkit on Next.js and Material UI, so each game stays smooth and in sync with the server's live outcomes.

cards.heading: Real-time game UIs
cards.body: Plinko, Mines, Cases, Battles, and Crash render live, driven by server events, with no desync between animation and outcome.

cards.heading: State that holds at scale
cards.body: A single Redux Toolkit store reflects live round state cleanly, staying responsive at 5k concurrent users.

result.head: A full casino of from-scratch games, live and in sync for up to 5k concurrent players. Private engagement.
after: Plinko, Mines, Cases, Battles, Crash, live

quote.text: Casino UIs are unforgiving: the animation and the server outcome have to agree, every round, for thousands of players at once. That sync is the whole job.
quote.name: Amit Rana

caseStudies.name: Felis AI

row.blurb: Built for a research scholar buried in papers: a RAG app where she uploads her research papers and queries across all of them in plain language.

caseStudies.tagline: Built for a research scholar drowning in papers. A RAG application where she uploads her research papers and asks questions across all of them in plain language.
caseStudies.context: Felis AI was built for Vanshika Tyagi, a research scholar who has to read through a large volume of research papers. The goal was a tool that lets a scholar hand their papers to an application and query across them in plain language, instead of re-reading everything by hand.

problem.head: Research scholars read a huge volume of papers, and finding the passage that answers a question means combing back through all of them by hand.
problem.body: A scholar's working set is dozens of dense papers. Keyword search misses paraphrased ideas, and there was no way to ask a question across the whole corpus and get a grounded answer that points back to the source.
problem.before: Dozens of papers to re-read · keyword search misses meaning · no cross-paper querying

approach.intro: I built a RAG application where a scholar uploads their papers and queries across them in natural language, with retrieval grounding every answer in the actual source and persistent memory across a research session.

cards.heading: Upload and query papers
cards.body: Scholars add their own research papers; the app retrieves the relevant passages and answers in plain language.

cards.heading: Grounded, with memory
cards.body: Every answer is grounded in the retrieved source, and persistent memory carries context across a long research session.

result.head: A research scholar can hand Felis her papers and ask questions across all of them, instead of re-reading by hand. Private engagement.
after: Upload papers, ask in plain language

quote.text: I read a huge number of research papers, and Felis changed how I work. I hand it my papers and ask questions across all of them instead of digging back through each one, and it points me to the right passage.
quote.name: Vanshika Tyagi
